    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "Genuinely interesting behavior \u2014 and it's actually faithful sim behavior, not a glitch. The difference between the two halves of the stroke comes down to one thing: **whether pigment had a continuous wet medium to bleed across, or only small patches of wet trapped inside dry paper.**\n\nWalking through it:\n\n**Bottom-left (the soft part).** Before you clicked Dry, the whole canvas was uniformly wet at 0.62 (the reset baseline). When the AI dropped pigment there, the surrounding cells were all wet, so pigment could diffuse and advect outward in every direction. Edge darkening pushed it toward the boundary of the wet region \u2014 but the \"boundary\" was the canvas edge, far away \u2014 so the effect was mild. The simulation ran for many frames before you clicked Dry, giving the pigment plenty of time to smooth out. By the time Dry hit, the soft falloff you see was already fully developed.\n\n**Top-right (the scaly part).** After Dry, every cell has `wet = 0`. The AI then continued its stroke. Each brush stamp does two things at the cells it touches: it deposits pigment in `g[]`, and it bumps `wet[i] += 0.45 * falloff`. That's enough to make the brush footprint locally \"wet enough to simulate\" \u2014 but only inside the brush radius, falling off toward zero at the edge. Outside the brush, the paper stays bone-dry.\n\nThis matters because the entire simulation gates on `wet[i] >= 0.04`. Edge darkening, the velocity update, pigment advection, pigment diffusion, and the transfer step all early-exit on dry cells. So pigment can move freely within the brush's wet footprint, but it cannot cross from wet to dry \u2014 there's an invisible fence at the edge of each stamp.\n\nThree things then conspire to create the scaly look:\n\n1. **Each brush stamp is its own little wet island.** Pigment deposited inside it can rearrange locally but can't escape into the surrounding dry paper. The boundary of the stamp is also the boundary of the simulation \u2014 sharp.\n2. **Edge darkening makes that boundary worse.** It's the \u00a74.3.3 outward-flow that creates \"ring\" effects in real watercolor. Normally that ring forms at the edge of a large wet wash. After Dry, every brush stamp is a wash, so every stamp grows its own little dark ring.\n3. **Consecutive stamps overlap but don't fully merge.** The AI paints one stamp per frame along a Bezier path. On wet paper those stamps would bleed into each other within a frame or two, becoming a continuous stroke. On dry paper, each stamp's wet zone partially overlaps the previous but only partially \u2014 you can see the individual circular footprints stacking like overlapping fish scales because that overlap-vs-non-overlap pattern is now permanent.\n\nThe same thing would happen in real watercolor too \u2014 wet-into-wet bleeds, wet-into-dry stays where you put it. The difference is that real paper has fiber-level capillary action that creates a soft halo around any stroke, even on bone-dry paper. The simulation has nothing like that \u2014 there's no \"spreading\" mechanism for wet itself, only for pigment within already-wet regions. So the simulation's wet-on-dry is harsher than real watercolor's wet-on-dry.\n\nIf you wanted to soften this, the cleanest fix would be to add a small diffusion of the `wet[]` array itself \u2014 so when paintAt locally re-wets a patch, the wet would slowly spread to neighbors over a few frames, creating the natural-looking halo before pigment caught up. That's a real sim change (and would slightly affect other behaviors too, like how fast a damp patch on dry paper spreads), but it would make the wet-on-dry case look much more like the wet-on-wet case. Let me know if you want it.",

    "type": "thinking",
    "thinking": " I'm planning v0.8 with a wet diffusion system where the moisture array itself spreads to neighboring areas over time, creating a natural halo effect before pigment can follow. The key challenge is determining the right diffusion rate to match real watercolor behavior.\n\nFor the simulation order, I need to place the wet diffusion step strategically\u2014probably after paint application but before pigment movement, so the pigment can follow the newly-spread moisture. The tricky part is deciding whether to diffuse wet before or after updating velocity and edge darkening, since the order affects which boundaries get darkened and how stable the simulation stays.\n\nI'm thinking a Laplacian-style diffusion approach makes sense here\u2014each cell averages with its neighbors to spread moisture outward naturally, which is exactly what we need for watercolor bleeding.\n\nFor the diffusion coefficient, I want something similar to the existing pigment diffusion rate of 0.045, maybe slightly faster at 0.06 or 0.08 to let the wet front propagate more aggressively than the pigment itself.\n\nActually, thinking about the physics here\u2014water should spread faster than pigment since water is what carries the pigment. So setting wet diffusion higher makes sense. I'll go with 0.08, which is about 1.7x the pigment rate.\n\nBut there's a problem with the dry-out logic: if wet diffuses outward to previously-dry cells, those cells get a small amount of wet that might fall below the evaporation threshold. Then evaporate runs and zeros them out, fighting against the diffusion I just added. I need to rethink how evaporation interacts with the newly-wetted cells.\n\nThe key insight is that the dry-out threshold is 0.025, so if diffusion brings a cell above that, it survives. With a brush stamp at 0.45 wet and a diffusion rate of 0.08, a single step should transfer about 0.036 to neighboring cells\u2014just enough to keep them alive.\n\nOver many frames though, the wet spreads thinner and thinner until cells eventually drop below the threshold and evaporate, which is the intended behavior for the expanding and fading halo effect.\n\nNow I'm thinking about how diffusion behaves at the canvas edges. With a Laplacian kernel, edge cells have fewer neighbors, so I need to decide on boundary conditions\u2014either treating the edge as zero wet (Dirichlet) or reflecting the wet value (Neumann). Looking at the existing code, it seems the diffusion only iterates over interior cells to avoid edge complications.\n\nI could apply the same approach to wet diffusion: skip the boundary cells entirely and let the overscan region handle any visual artifacts. Then I'm wondering if I can combine the wet diffusion with the existing pigment diffusion pass\u2014since pigment already skips cells below a threshold, I could diffuse both wet and pigment in a single iteration.\n\nThe tradeoff is that fusing them would require copying both arrays to temporary buffers before the pass, but the tighter cache behavior might offset the extra work. The real question is whether wet diffusion should also respect the pigment threshold or operate independently.\n\nI'm realizing the two-pass approach makes more sense: wet diffuses everywhere on interior cells first, then pigment diffuses only where it's wet enough. But that creates a timing issue\u2014newly-wet cells from the first pass won't see pigment diffusion until the next step. The issue is that lowering the pigment gate threshold to match the evaporation threshold (0.025) would let pigment follow water into the barely-wet halo cells, but this gate is used throughout the code in multiple places, so changing it has wider implications than just the pigment movement.\n\nInstead, I should tune the wet diffusion rate itself so that the halo carries enough moisture to naturally cross the existing 0.04 threshold. With a diffusion rate of 0.08 and a brush wet level of 0.45, a neighbor cell would receive about 0.036 wet per timestep\u2014just barely below the 0.04 gate, so I need to push the diffusion rate higher to reliably trigger pigment movement in the halo.\n\nBumping WET_DIFFUSION to 0.1 means a neighbor cell gets 0.045 wet per timestep, which clears the threshold. The Laplacian diffusion calculation spreads moisture from the wet center outward to its neighbors, so even a dry neighbor adjacent to the wet brush center will accumulate enough moisture to activate pigment transport. Let me trace through a concrete example with a wet cell surrounded by dry neighbors to verify the diffusion math is conserving water correctly\u2014the center cell loses 0.18 units while each of the four neighbors gains 0.045, which checks out.\n\nThe wet front propagates outward at roughly one cell per step, so after a few simulation steps the moisture has spread several cells away from the source. As the water diffuses outward, the concentration at the center drops, and eventually the entire field falls below the activation threshold and the wet region dissipates.\n\nNow I'm thinking through the computational cost of the diffusion calculation itself.\n\nAt 572k cells with 120 simulation steps per second, the wet diffusion alone accounts for about 412 million operations per second. This pushes the total per-step work from around 16N to 22N operations, which is a 37% increase. That's significant enough to consider optimizations like only diffusing near the wet-dry boundary or skipping cells where the Laplacian would be zero anyway\u2014when a cell and all its neighbors have equal wet values, nothing changes, so we could short-circuit that calculation.\n\nWriting optimization logic... I'm adding an optimization to skip cells where the center and all four neighbors are below a tiny threshold like 1e-6, since the Laplacian would be negligible anyway and this check is much faster than computing the full diffusion math.\n\nThere's a trade-off here \u2014 five comparisons versus the cost of the Laplacian calculation itself, which probably breaks even when most cells are wet but saves a lot when the canvas is mostly dry.\n\nI'm considering a different approach: tracking a bounding box of wet cells and only iterating within that region, expanding it when new paint is added and shrinking it as moisture evaporates.\n\nFor now though, I'll keep it simple for v0.8 \u2014 just add the diffusion pass with a per-cell skip when all five values are negligible, and I'll need to figure out which temporary array to use for the wet copy since g_tmp is already allocated for pigment.\n\nLooking at the existing temp arrays, wetBlurTmp gets overwritten during edge darkening which runs before wet diffusion in the simulation step, so I can't reuse it. The cleanest approach is to allocate a dedicated wet_tmp array \u2014 it's only 4MB at the current grid dimensions, so the memory cost is minimal.\n\nNow I'm working through where wet diffusion should fit in the simStep sequence. If I run it before edge darkening, the wet field expands first and then everything else operates on that expanded state, which feels most consistent. But I need to think through how this interacts with paintAt, which gets called from applyPendingPaint before simStep even runs.\n\nSo the flow would be: paintAt bumps the wet locally, then in the next simStep the wet diffuses immediately, followed by edge darkening, velocity updates, pigment movement, and transfer all using that new wet field. That means a paint stroke gets diffused right away in the same frame.\n\nFor naming, I'm leaning toward `diffuseWet` as a separate function rather than inlining it, keeping it consistent with the existing naming pattern like `applyEdgeDarkening`.\n\nOn making it tunable\u2014the user has been asking for adjustable parameters like fade halflife, but wet diffusion feels more like a physics constant than a creative effect. I'll keep it as a fixed constant for now in v0.8.\n\nAdding wet diffusion will subtly enhance the existing behavior: wet-on-wet painting spreads a bit more before settling, the wet region shrinks as edges dry below threshold, and the overall feel becomes softer. I should probably make it toggleable so users can compare the effect.\n\nActually, for v0.8 I'll just add it without a toggle since the user didn't ask for one\u2014they can request it later if needed. Now let me implement: I'll add the diffusion constant, allocate a temporary buffer, write the diffusion function, call it early in the simulation step, and bump the version. Let me also double-check the math\u2014with a diffusion coefficient of 0.10, a brush stamp at 0.45 wet level transfers about 0.045 to neighbors per step, which stays safely above the threshold.\n\nThe brush has falloff though, so at the edges the wet level might only be 0.05, barely above threshold. I should probably increase the diffusion coefficient to 0.12 or 0.15 to get better spreading. Let me verify the stability: 0.15 times 4 neighbors equals 0.6, which is well under 1, so that's stable.\n\nActually, the stability bound for explicit diffusion on a 4-neighbor grid is 0.25 maximum, so I can safely go up to 0.15 for good halo formation. But wait, let me double-check my math on how a dry neighboring cell actually becomes wet through diffusion.\n\nIf cell B starts dry and sits next to a wet cell A at 0.45, after one step B should gain 0.0675 wetness, which crosses the threshold. Now I'm tracing through what happens in the second step\u2014A loses diffusion to its neighbors while potentially gaining from them, and I need to verify the net effect on A's wetness value. That's pretty aggressive\u2014after just a couple more steps, the center could drop below the 0.04 threshold and stop diffusing entirely. Let me test smaller diffusion rates to find a sweet spot. With 0.08, the neighbors barely miss the gate, but 0.10 gets them just above it, so that's my target. The center drops from 0.45 to 0.27 in one step, which feels more reasonable.\n\nNow I'm mentally simulating how this plays out over several steps\u2014the wet spreads outward as a halo, values settle into the 0.04-0.1 range, and anything that dips below 0.025 evaporates. Let me trace through this properly in 2D with the center starting at 0.45 surrounded by zeros. I'm getting bogged down in the neighbor calculations\u2014the geometry gets messy when tracking which cells have been updated at each step. Let me just run with a diffusion coefficient of 0.10 and see how it behaves; by step 5 the wetness should spread outward in roughly a Gaussian pattern, which seems reasonable for this kind of diffusion simulation.\n\nNow I'm realizing the threshold gate at 0.04 might be too aggressive\u2014with diffusion active, cells will hover around that boundary for a while and could flicker between wet and dry states, creating artifacts. I could lower the threshold to something like 0.015 when diffusion is enabled, or skip the gate entirely for certain operations. The gate serves two purposes: it's a performance optimization to skip dry cells, but it also enforces physical correctness since pigment shouldn't move on completely dry paper. The tricky part is balancing those two concerns without introducing discontinuities.\n\nIf I tune the wet diffusion coefficient properly\u2014say 0.10 with a brush wet of 0.45\u2014I get about 0.045 on the first step, which barely clears the 0.04 threshold. But as wet spreads outward in subsequent steps, the edge cells receive smaller increments each time, and the wet front only propagates about one cell per simulation step.\n\nActually, this approach feels too fragile. Let me bump the coefficient up to 0.15 instead to guarantee the halo forms reliably. Running through the math: the brush cell loses 0.27 wet on step one while neighbors gain 0.0675, then on step two the brush cell continues losing wet as diffusion pulls it back down toward equilibrium, eventually dropping below 0.025. Meanwhile the neighbors keep climbing as more wet flows in from the brush region.\n\nIn steady state, the wet redistributes around the brush and some cells dip below the drying threshold while others hover near it. But since evaporation's also running, the whole system gradually loses moisture over time\u2014the halo forms and then slowly fades away, which actually looks realistic. I'll go with 0.10 as the final coefficient and let the user request tweaks if needed. One last consideration: should I gate the diffusion to only run where there's actually wet present? If a cell and all its neighbors are dry, the Laplacian is zero anyway, so skipping that calculation would save unnecessary work.\n\nImplementing the diffusion function with the optimization to skip cells where there's no wet in the neighborhood... I'm thinking through what wet diffusion actually changes across the whole simulation\u2014it's not just a visual effect for wet-on-dry, but affects how pigment behaves during wet-on-wet blending, how the wet region expands slightly as water spreads before evaporating, and gives brush strokes softer, more organic edges overall. The performance cost is noticeable at around 37% more work per simulation step, so I should consider optimization strategies like skipping diffusion when the wet region is too small to matter.\n\nFor a uniformly wet canvas right after reset, the Laplacian is essentially zero since all neighboring cells have equal values, so the diffusion math produces no actual change. I could add a check that skips the calculation when the center and all neighbors are nearly equal, which would catch this case and save unnecessary computation.\n\nImplementing the optimization check... I'm skipping the optimization check for now and just implementing the straightforward diffusion\u2014if performance becomes an issue I can optimize later. My plan is to bump the version to 0.8, add the wet_tmp array and WET_DIFFUSION constant, implement the diffuseWet function, call it at the start of simStep, and then do a sanity check on the parsing before explaining the effect to the user. Now I need to figure out where to allocate wet_tmp and place the diffuseWet function.\n\nBut there's a wrinkle with the user's test case: they pause drying and use the Dry button to zero out the wet array, then the AI paints wet values around 0.45. Once diffusion kicks in, the wet halo spreads outward, but with drying paused it never drops below threshold\u2014it just keeps expanding until the wet value equilibrates across the whole canvas. Since diffusion conserves total wet without evaporation, a small painted stamp will eventually spread into an imperceptibly thin layer across the entire canvas.\n\nThis behavior is probably acceptable\u2014the halo grows slowly and might not be noticeable. If the user finds it problematic, they can simply turn off Pause drying to let evaporation handle it naturally. I'll go ahead and implement this.",

    "text": "Implementing in v0.8. The physics: a Laplacian diffusion on the `wet[]` array, run at the top of each simStep before everything else. Each cell looks at its 4 neighbors, water flows down the gradient. Real watercolor's capillary spread isn't strictly Laplacian, but it's a good approximation for our purposes \u2014 and it's the same shape as the pigment diffusion already in the code, so it'll be cheap to add.\n\nTuning is the interesting part. The coefficient needs to be high enough that newly-wet neighbor cells cross the `wet >= 0.04` activity threshold in one step (otherwise the halo would form but the sim wouldn't notice). With paintAt depositing 0.45 at the brush center, a coefficient of 0.10 transfers 0.045 to each neighbor per step \u2014 just above the threshold. Sets a nice floor.\n\nLet me find the state arrays and simStep:",

    "input": {
      "description": "Add diffuseWet \u2014 Laplacian diffusion on the wet[] array. Each cell averages with its 4 neighbors via the standard explicit-Euler diffusion step. Conserves total wet (mass-conserving for an isolated system). The skip-on-all-zero short-circuit avoids work in fully dry regions, which is most of the canvas in a typical painting.",
      "new_str": "// ============================================================\n// WET DIFFUSION (added v0.8) \u2014 water spreads to adjacent cells\n// over time, modeling capillary action through paper fibers.\n// ============================================================\n// Without this, paintAt's wet bump stays confined to the brush\n// footprint, so on dry paper each brush stamp creates an island\n// of wet trapped inside dry surroundings. Pigment can move within\n// the island but can't cross the wet\u2192dry boundary, so brush stamps\n// show as hard-edged stamps. With wet diffusion, the wet itself\n// expands outward, creating a soft halo ahead of the pigment so\n// dry-paper strokes get the same soft falloff as wet-on-wet strokes.\n//\n// Standard Laplacian diffusion: each cell exchanges with its 4\n// neighbors based on the local gradient. Conserves total wet\n// (in the interior); boundary cells are skipped. Coefficient 0.10\n// is tuned so that a 0.45-wet brush center transfers 0.045 to each\n// neighbor per step, just clearing the wet >= 0.04 threshold so\n// the rest of the sim picks up those cells as active. Stability\n// bound for an explicit 4-neighbor scheme is 0.25; we're well under.\nconst WET_DIFFUSION = 0.10;\n\nfunction diffuseWet() {\n  wet_tmp.set(wet);\n  const k = WET_DIFFUSION;\n  for (let y = 1; y < GH - 1; y++) {\n    const yo = y * GW;\n    for (let x = 1; x < GW - 1; x++) {\n      const i = yo + x;\n      // Skip cells with no wet anywhere in the 5-cell stencil \u2014 the\n      // Laplacian is zero, no change would result. This is the common\n      // case in unpainted areas.\n      const c  = wet_tmp[i];\n      const wa = wet_tmp[i - 1],  wb = wet_tmp[i + 1];\n      const wc = wet_tmp[i - GW], wd = wet_tmp[i + GW];\n      if (c < 1e-6 && wa < 1e-6 && wb < 1e-6 && wc < 1e-6 && wd < 1e-6) continue;\n      wet[i] = c + k * (wa + wb + wc + wd - 4 * c);\n    }\n  }\n}\n\nfunction simStep() {\n  diffuseWet();\n  if (edgeDarkeningEnabled) applyEdgeDarkening();\n  updateVelocity();\n  movePigment();\n  transferPigment();\n  if (!dryingPaused) evaporate();\n}",
      "old_str": "function simStep() {\n  if (edgeDarkeningEnabled) applyEdgeDarkening();\n  updateVelocity();\n  movePigment();\n  transferPigment();\n  if (!dryingPaused) evaporate();\n}",
      "path": "/home/claude/watercolor.html"
    },
    "message": "Add diffuseWet \u2014 Laplacian diffusion on the wet[] array. Each cell averages with its 4 neighbors via the standard explicit-Euler diffusion step.     "start_timestamp": "2026-05-11T02:59:57.085407Z",

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "v0.8. The wet diffusion runs at the top of every simStep, before edge darkening and the rest. The painting you described should now look much closer end-to-end \u2014 the post-Dry section will still be subtly different from the pre-Dry section, but the brush stamp footprints should soften considerably as their wet halos race ahead of the pigment.\n\nA few specific behaviors to expect:\n\n**Wet-on-dry now develops a halo.** When you paint on dry paper, each brush stamp's wet bump immediately starts spreading to neighboring cells. Pigment follows the wet (since the per-cell `wet >= 0.04` gate now lets diffusion-fed cells pass), so what used to be a hard-edged stamp now gets a soft surrounding wet zone that pigment can drift into.\n\n**Wet-on-wet is barely affected.** When the entire canvas is uniformly wet, the Laplacian is effectively zero everywhere \u2014 neighboring cells already have the same wet level, so there's no gradient to flow down. Diffusion silently does nothing. So your familiar bleed behavior on wet paper is unchanged.\n\n**The \"leading edge\" effect.** Real watercolor on dry paper does this beautifully: the water front races a step ahead of the pigment front, creating a clean, slightly damp halo around the stroke before any pigment arrives there. The simulation now does the same thing \u2014 diffusion pushes wet outward by ~1 cell per simStep at the front, and pigment movement follows in the cells that have become wet enough.\n\n**Drying interaction.** The diffusion spreads the budget of total wet thinner and thinner. With evaporation running normally, this means the halo expands a bit AND evaporates away \u2014 same as real paper. With Pause drying on, total wet is conserved, so a small wet patch will eventually diffuse toward a thin uniform film across the whole canvas (extremely slowly \u2014 the equilibrium of 0.45 wet over 572k cells is ~1e-6 per cell). In practice you'd never notice unless you waited hours.\n\n**Performance cost.** Adds one Laplacian pass per simStep. With the dry-region short-circuit (skip when all 5 cells in the stencil are below 1e-6), this is essentially free on unpainted regions and roughly 6N ops on painted regions. Active simStep cost goes from ~16N to ~22N \u2014 about 37% more work when painting heavily, basically unchanged when idle (since simStep doesn't run at all then).\n\nIf 0.10 turns out to be too aggressive (wet halo races too far ahead of pigment) or too gentle (still see brush stamps on dry paper), the constant is one line near the top of the function. 0.06 would slow the halo to about 60% the current spread rate; 0.15 would speed it up significantly without crossing the 0.25 stability bound.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

      "json_block": "{\"language\": \"html\", \"code\": \"function fadeStep() {\\n  const f = fadeFactor;\\n  const d0 = d[0], d1 = d[1], d2 = d[2];\\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\\n    d0[i] *= f;\\n    d1[i] *= f;\\n    d2[i] *= f;\\n  }\\n}\\n\\nfunction loop() {\\n  applyPendingPaint();\\n  aiStep();\\n  framesSincePaint++;\\n  framesSinceIdleCheck++;\\n  framesSinceFade++;\\n\\n  const runSim = shouldRunSim();\\n  if (runSim) {\\n    simStep();\\n    simStep();\\n  }\\n\\n  // Fade tick: 10 Hz when enabled, skipped entirely when the toggle is\\n  // off OR the canvas has effectively faded to blank \\u2014 no point spending\\n  // cycles multiplying near-zeroes.\\n  let didFade = false;\\n  if (fadeEnabled && framesSinceFade >= FADE_TICK_INTERVAL) {\\n    framesSinceFade = 0;\\n    if (currentAvgPigment > FADE_SKIP_BELOW) {\\n      fadeStep();\\n      didFade = true;\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n\", \"filename\": \"/home/claude/watercolor.html\"}"

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Good. Now I have everything I need. Let me implement.\n\n**Step 1:** Add the `RAINBOW_INDEX` sentinel and the cycle math next to the other tool sentinels:",

  {

    "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Add RAINBOW_INDEX sentinel + cycle math. Period 1500ms, 500ms per stop. Weights stored in a shared array to avoid per-call allocation since updateRainbowWeights is called both per-frame for the cursor and per-paintAt-call for the deposit.",
      "new_str": "const WATER_INDEX = -1;\n// A sentinel for the lift (subtract) brush \u2014 removes pigment from the\n// paper at touched cells, leaving water and pressure alone. The water\n// brush already lifts in the sense of re-suspending d \u2192 g, but it\n// doesn't reduce the total pigment on the page; this one does.\nconst LIFT_INDEX  = -2;\n// A sentinel for the rainbow brush \u2014 deposits a time-varying mixture\n// of all three pigments. The deposit weights cycle rose \u2192 yellow \u2192\n// blue \u2192 rose with one stop every RAINBOW_STOP_MS, so a continuous\n// stroke smears through the full pigment cycle. See rainbowWeights().\nconst RAINBOW_INDEX = -3;\nconst RAINBOW_PERIOD_MS = 1500;\nconst RAINBOW_STOP_MS   = 500;\n// Shared output array to avoid per-call allocation. Read after each\n// updateRainbowWeights call.\nconst rainbowW = [0, 0, 0];\nfunction updateRainbowWeights(t_ms) {\n  // Three linear segments: rose\u2192yellow, yellow\u2192blue, blue\u2192rose.\n  // The wraparound (blue\u2192rose) closes the loop so a continuous stroke\n  // doesn't have a discontinuity at the cycle boundary.\n  const t = t_ms % RAINBOW_PERIOD_MS;\n  if (t < RAINBOW_STOP_MS) {\n    const f = t / RAINBOW_STOP_MS;\n    rainbowW[0] = 1 - f; rainbowW[1] = f; rainbowW[2] = 0;\n  } else if (t < 2 * RAINBOW_STOP_MS) {\n    const f = (t - RAINBOW_STOP_MS) / RAINBOW_STOP_MS;\n    rainbowW[0] = 0; rainbowW[1] = 1 - f; rainbowW[2] = f;\n  } else {\n    const f = (t - 2 * RAINBOW_STOP_MS) / RAINBOW_STOP_MS;\n    rainbowW[0] = f; rainbowW[1] = 0; rainbowW[2] = 1 - f;\n  }\n}\nlet currentPigment = 0;",
      "old_str": "const WATER_INDEX = -1;\n// A sentinel for the lift (subtract) brush \u2014 removes pigment from the\n// paper at touched cells, leaving water and pressure alone. The water\n// brush already lifts in the sense of re-suspending d \u2192 g, but it\n// doesn't reduce the total pigment on the page; this one does.\nconst LIFT_INDEX  = -2;\nlet currentPigment = 0;",

    },

      "description": "Add rainbow branch to paintAt. Same loop shape as the regular pigment branch but deposits across all three g[] arrays weighted by the current rainbow cycle position. wet/pressure bumps match the regular branch exactly so a rainbow stroke feels identical in flow dynamics to a single-pigment stroke.",
      "new_str": "        if (subFrac <= 0) continue;\n        const keep = 1 - subFrac;\n        for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n          g[k][i] *= keep;\n          d[k][i] *= keep;\n        }\n      }\n    }\n    return;\n  }\n\n  // Rainbow brush \u2014 same dynamics as a regular pigment brush, but the\n  // strength is split across all three g[] arrays weighted by the\n  // current cycle position. Weights are computed once per paintAt call\n  // (not per cell), so every cell in this stamp gets the same color\n  // mix; the color cycles between stamps over time.\n  if (pigmentIdx === RAINBOW_INDEX) {\n    updateRainbowWeights(performance.now());\n    const w0 = rainbowW[0], w1 = rainbowW[1], w2 = rainbowW[2];\n    const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\n    for (let py = minY; py <= maxY; py++) {\n      for (let px = minX; px <= maxX; px++) {\n        const dx = px - gx, dy = py - gy;\n        const d2 = dx * dx + dy * dy;\n        if (d2 > r2) continue;\n        const i = py * GW + px;\n        const falloff = 1 - Math.sqrt(d2) / r;\n        const f2 = falloff * falloff;\n        const add = strength * f2;\n        // Per-pigment deposit, weighted. Each pigment is independently\n        // clamped to MAX_PIGMENT \u2014 a saturated cell of one pigment\n        // doesn't block deposits of the other two.\n        let na = g0[i] + add * w0; if (na > MAX_PIGMENT) na = MAX_PIGMENT; g0[i] = na;\n            na = g1[i] + add * w1; if (na > MAX_PIGMENT) na = MAX_PIGMENT; g1[i] = na;\n            na = g2[i] + add * w2; if (na > MAX_PIGMENT) na = MAX_PIGMENT; g2[i] = na;\n        const ww = wet[i] + 0.45 * falloff;\n        wet[i] = ww > 1 ? 1 : ww;\n        pressure[i] += 0.18 * f2;\n      }\n    }\n    return;\n  }\n\n  const arr = g[pigmentIdx];",
      "old_str": "        if (subFrac <= 0) continue;\n        const keep = 1 - subFrac;\n        for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n          g[k][i] *= keep;\n          d[k][i] *= keep;\n        }\n      }\n    }\n    return;\n  }\n\n  const arr = g[pigmentIdx];",

  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-11T03:10:58.874352Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-11T03:11:13.941736Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "tool_use",
    "id": "toolu_01XJYzZyrDFjHyYuZoPxXJJT",
    "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Add a rainbow branch to applyCursorColor that computes KM with weighted K/S per channel. Also add a tiny rainbow-only update function for the loop to call every frame; this is the cheap version that just updates the cursor background without redoing border calculations.",
      "new_str": "function applyCursorColor() {\n  if (currentPigment === WATER_INDEX) {\n    // Water cursor: near-transparent fill, cool-gray outline.\n    cursor.style.background = 'rgba(180, 210, 220, 0.18)';\n    cursor.style.border = '1.5px solid rgba(110, 145, 160, 0.65)';\n    return;\n  }\n  if (currentPigment === LIFT_INDEX) {\n    // Lift cursor: faint warm fill, dashed charcoal outline to signal\n    // a subtractive action (vs the solid outlines used by adding tools).\n    cursor.style.background = 'rgba(245, 240, 232, 0.22)';\n    cursor.style.border = '1.5px dashed rgba(95, 85, 75, 0.78)';\n    return;\n  }\n  if (currentPigment === RAINBOW_INDEX) {\n    // Rainbow cursor: time-varying color. Set a placeholder here; the\n    // main loop calls updateRainbowCursor() each frame to keep it in\n    // sync with the cycle.\n    cursor.style.border = '1.5px solid rgba(95, 85, 75, 0.55)';\n    updateRainbowCursor();\n    return;\n  }\n  const p = PIGMENTS[currentPigment];\n  // Render a representative color using KM with a thin layer for the cursor fill\n  const R = kmReflect(p.K[0], p.S[0], 0.6, PAPER_R_BASE);\n  const G = kmReflect(p.K[1], p.S[1], 0.6, PAPER_G_BASE);\n  const B = kmReflect(p.K[2], p.S[2], 0.6, PAPER_B_BASE);\n  const r = Math.round(R * 255), gg = Math.round(G * 255), b = Math.round(B * 255);\n  cursor.style.background = `rgba(${r},${gg},${b},0.35)`;\n  cursor.style.border = `1.5px solid rgba(${Math.round(r*0.55)},${Math.round(gg*0.55)},${Math.round(b*0.55)},0.85)`;\n}\n\n// Lightweight per-frame cursor update for rainbow brush. Reuses\n// rainbowW (set by updateRainbowWeights) and uses the same KM math\n// applyCursorColor does for regular pigments, but with K and S\n// computed as the weighted blend of the three pigments' values.\nfunction updateRainbowCursor() {\n  updateRainbowWeights(performance.now());\n  const w0 = rainbowW[0], w1 = rainbowW[1], w2 = rainbowW[2];\n  const P0 = PIGMENTS[0], P1 = PIGMENTS[1], P2 = PIGMENTS[2];\n  const Kr = P0.K[0]*w0 + P1.K[0]*w1 + P2.K[0]*w2;\n  const Kg = P0.K[1]*w0 + P1.K[1]*w1 + P2.K[1]*w2;\n  const Kb = P0.K[2]*w0 + P1.K[2]*w1 + P2.K[2]*w2;\n  const Sr = P0.S[0]*w0 + P1.S[0]*w1 + P2.S[0]*w2;\n  const Sg = P0.S[1]*w0 + P1.S[1]*w1 + P2.S[1]*w2;\n  const Sb = P0.S[2]*w0 + P1.S[2]*w1 + P2.S[2]*w2;\n  const R = kmReflect(Kr, Sr, 0.6, PAPER_R_BASE);\n  const G = kmReflect(Kg, Sg, 0.6, PAPER_G_BASE);\n  const B = kmReflect(Kb, Sb, 0.6, PAPER_B_BASE);\n  const r  = Math.round(R * 255);\n  const gg = Math.round(G * 255);\n  const b  = Math.round(B * 255);\n  cursor.style.background = `rgba(${r},${gg},${b},0.45)`;\n}",
      "old_str": "function applyCursorColor() {\n  if (currentPigment === WATER_INDEX) {\n    // Water cursor: near-transparent fill, cool-gray outline.\n    cursor.style.background = 'rgba(180, 210, 220, 0.18)';\n    cursor.style.border = '1.5px solid rgba(110, 145, 160, 0.65)';\n    return;\n  }\n  if (currentPigment === LIFT_INDEX) {\n    // Lift cursor: faint warm fill, dashed charcoal outline to signal\n    // a subtractive action (vs the solid outlines used by adding tools).\n    cursor.style.background = 'rgba(245, 240, 232, 0.22)';\n    cursor.style.border = '1.5px dashed rgba(95, 85, 75, 0.78)';\n    return;\n  }\n  const p = PIGMENTS[currentPigment];\n  // Render a representative color using KM with a thin layer for the cursor fill\n  const R = kmReflect(p.K[0], p.S[0], 0.6, PAPER_R_BASE);\n  const G = kmReflect(p.K[1], p.S[1], 0.6, PAPER_G_BASE);\n  const B = kmReflect(p.K[2], p.S[2], 0.6, PAPER_B_BASE);\n  const r = Math.round(R * 255), gg = Math.round(G * 255), b = Math.round(B * 255);\n  cursor.style.background = `rgba(${r},${gg},${b},0.35)`;\n  cursor.style.border = `1.5px solid rgba(${Math.round(r*0.55)},${Math.round(gg*0.55)},${Math.round(b*0.55)},0.85)`;\n}",
      "path": "/home/claude/watercolor.html"
    },
    "message": "Add a rainbow branch to applyCursorColor that computes KM with weighted K/S per channel. Also add a tiny rainbow-only update function for the loop to call every frame; this is the cheap version that just updates the cursor background without redoing border calculations.",

    "text": "**Step 5:** Add the rainbow swatch at the end of `buildPigmentSwatches`. I'll model it on the Lift swatch shape (circular, 56px canvas) but render a horizontal sweep through the three KM-rendered pigment colors:",
    "citations": []
  },
  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-11T03:11:24.269554Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-11T03:11:38.263798Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "tool_use",
    "id": "toolu_01SdvTwxekSCu2c5SiNV9pmE",
    "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Add the rainbow swatch after Lift. Renders a horizontal sweep through the three KM-rendered pigment colors using the same per-x time-equivalent mapping the cursor uses. The speckle and the circular mask match the other swatches' aesthetic.",
      "new_str": "    wrap.addEventListener('click', () => {\n      currentPigment = LIFT_INDEX;\n      document.querySelectorAll('.pigment').forEach(el => el.classList.remove('active'));\n      wrap.classList.add('active');\n      applyCursorColor();\n    });\n\n    root.appendChild(wrap);\n  }\n\n  // Rainbow swatch \u2014 horizontal sweep through the three pigments\n  // using the same KM math that paintAt's rainbow branch will deposit.\n  // Shows rose at the left edge through yellow in the middle to blue\n  // at the right edge; the blue\u2192rose wrap part of the cycle is not\n  // drawn here since the user can already see all three primaries.\n  {\n    const wrap = document.createElement('div');\n    wrap.className = 'pigment' + (currentPigment === RAINBOW_INDEX ? ' active' : '');\n    wrap.title = 'Rainbow \u2014 cycles rose \u2192 yellow \u2192 blue \u2192 rose over 1.5s';\n    wrap.dataset.idx = RAINBOW_INDEX;\n\n    const swatchDiv = document.createElement('div');\n    swatchDiv.className = 'pigment-swatch';\n\n    const sc = document.createElement('canvas');\n    sc.width = 56; sc.height = 56;\n    const sctx = sc.getContext('2d');\n    const sd = sctx.createImageData(56, 56);\n    const P0 = PIGMENTS[0], P1 = PIGMENTS[1], P2 = PIGMENTS[2];\n    for (let y = 0; y < 56; y++) {\n      for (let x = 0; x < 56; x++) {\n        const dx = x - 28, dy = y - 28;\n        const d2 = dx*dx + dy*dy;\n        const j = (y * 56 + x) * 4;\n        if (d2 > 28*28) {\n          sd.data[j] = 0; sd.data[j+1] = 0; sd.data[j+2] = 0; sd.data[j+3] = 0;\n          continue;\n        }\n        // Map x position to first 2/3 of the rainbow cycle so we get\n        // rose \u2192 yellow \u2192 blue across the swatch width. Three weights\n        // computed inline with the same shape as updateRainbowWeights\n        // but parameterized by x instead of time.\n        const frac = x / 55; // 0..1 across width\n        let w0, w1, w2;\n        if (frac < 0.5) {\n          const f = frac / 0.5;\n          w0 = 1 - f; w1 = f; w2 = 0;\n        } else {\n          const f = (frac - 0.5) / 0.5;\n          w0 = 0; w1 = 1 - f; w2 = f;\n        }\n        const Kr = P0.K[0]*w0 + P1.K[0]*w1 + P2.K[0]*w2;\n        const Kg = P0.K[1]*w0 + P1.K[1]*w1 + P2.K[1]*w2;\n        const Kb = P0.K[2]*w0 + P1.K[2]*w1 + P2.K[2]*w2;\n        const Sr = P0.S[0]*w0 + P1.S[0]*w1 + P2.S[0]*w2;\n        const Sg = P0.S[1]*w0 + P1.S[1]*w1 + P2.S[1]*w2;\n        const Sb = P0.S[2]*w0 + P1.S[2]*w1 + P2.S[2]*w2;\n        // Slight thickness variation by y for an organic swatch feel,\n        // matching the speckle treatment of the pigment swatches.\n        const thickness = 0.55 + 0.4 * smoothNoise(x * 0.3, y * 0.3);\n        const R = kmReflect(Kr, Sr, thickness, PAPER_R_BASE);\n        const G = kmReflect(Kg, Sg, thickness, PAPER_G_BASE);\n        const B = kmReflect(Kb, Sb, thickness, PAPER_B_BASE);\n        sd.data[j]     = Math.min(255, Math.max(0, R * 255));\n        sd.data[j + 1] = Math.min(255, Math.max(0, G * 255));\n        sd.data[j + 2] = Math.min(255, Math.max(0, B * 255));\n        sd.data[j + 3] = 255;\n      }\n    }\n    sctx.putImageData(sd, 0, 0);\n    swatchDiv.appendChild(sc);\n\n    const lbl = document.createElement('div');\n    lbl.className = 'pigment-label';\n    lbl.innerHTML = 'Rainbow';\n\n    wrap.appendChild(swatchDiv);\n    wrap.appendChild(lbl);\n\n    wrap.addEventListener('click', () => {\n      currentPigment = RAINBOW_INDEX;\n      document.querySelectorAll('.pigment').forEach(el => el.classList.remove('active'));\n      wrap.classList.add('active');\n      applyCursorColor();\n    });\n\n    root.appendChild(wrap);\n  }\n}",
      "old_str": "    wrap.addEventListener('click', () => {\n      currentPigment = LIFT_INDEX;\n      document.querySelectorAll('.pigment').forEach(el => el.classList.remove('active'));\n      wrap.classList.add('active');\n      applyCursorColor();\n    });\n\n    root.appendChild(wrap);\n  }\n}",
      "path": "/home/claude/watercolor.html"
    },

    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-11T03:12:05.630935Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "v0.8.1. Rainbow swatch sits at the end of the row, after Lift. Click it and start dragging \u2014 you'll see the cursor color cycle through rose \u2192 yellow \u2192 blue \u2192 rose every 1.5 seconds while the brush leaves a smooth color gradient behind on the canvas.\n\nA few design choices worth knowing about:\n\n**Weights computed once per `paintAt` call, not per cell.** Every cell in a single brush stamp shares the same color mix. The color cycle happens over many frames; within one stamp it'd be visually identical anyway since stamps are ~5ms apart in clock time and the cycle is 1500ms long. This keeps the per-cell work in the rainbow branch the same shape as the regular pigment branch.\n\n**Deposits distribute across all three `g[]` arrays.** Each cell gets `strength * falloff * weight[k]` added to `g[k]` for k in 0..2. Per-pigment MAX_PIGMENT clamping is independent, so saturating cell with one pigment doesn't block the other two from being deposited. The total deposit (`strength * falloff`) is identical to a single-pigment brush \u2014 so a rainbow stroke \"feels\" the same in flow dynamics.\n\n**Wet, pressure, and edge darkening behavior all match the regular pigment brush.** Same 0.45 wet bump, same 0.18 pressure bump. So a rainbow stroke bleeds and edge-darkens like any other.\n\n**Cursor updates every frame.** `updateRainbowCursor` recomputes weighted K/S coefficients across the three pigments, runs `kmReflect` per channel, sets `cursor.style.background`. About 3 kmReflect calls (each one sinh, one cosh, one divide) per frame when rainbow is selected. Around 1-2\u00b5s total \u2014 invisible cost. The update runs even when the simulation is idle, so the cursor cycles independently of any painting activity.\n\n**Phase is wall-clock based.** Uses `performance.now() % 1500`, so the phase is global \u2014 switching to another brush and back won't reset the cycle. If you paint a stroke, switch to Water, switch back, the rainbow keeps marching.\n\n**Swatch shows a static rose\u2192yellow\u2192blue gradient (left to right).** I rendered the gradient using the same KM math the brush deposits, so the swatch is a true preview of what the brush will draw \u2014 not a CSS gradient approximation. It only shows the first 2/3 of the cycle (the rose\u2192yellow\u2192blue half) rather than animating; the blue\u2192rose wraparound segment isn't drawn because seeing all three primaries in one strip reads more cleanly as \"this is a multi-pigment brush\" than an animated loop would.\n\n**AI painter doesn't use rainbow yet.** I left the AI strategies list alone \u2014 the AI keeps picking from rose/yellow/blue/water/lift only. Adding rainbow to its mix would be one line (`{ name: 'rainbow', weight: 1.0, brushPx: [20, 60], dist: [0.10, 0.30], dur: [30, 60], strength: 0.34, pigment: RAINBOW_INDEX }`), but felt like a separate creative decision. Easy to add if you want \u2014 let me know.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

    "type": "text",
    "text": "v0.9 at native resolution. Each grid cell is now exactly one display pixel \u2014 no upscaling in the display path at all. At a 1920\u00d71080 viewport with the 1.05 overscan, that works out to ~2.3M cells (vs. ~570k at v0.8). \n\nWhat you'll notice visually:\n\n**Finer everything.** Granulation specks, pigment edges, paper texture, edge-darkening rings \u2014 all rendered at twice the linear resolution. Subtle details that were getting averaged across a 2\u00d72 cell footprint at v0.8 now resolve individually. The pigment edges should look noticeably crisper at the brush boundary, and the rainbow brush's color transitions read as smoother gradients.\n\n**Pigment diffuses slightly slower in display-pixel terms.** This is the subtle interaction with the resolution bump: `PIGMENT_DIFFUSION = 0.045` is per grid cell, so the pigment still spreads at \"1 cell per few steps\" \u2014 but since cells are now half as wide in display pixels, the visible bleed rate is halved. The effect is a slightly tighter, more controlled-feeling stroke. Same applies to `WET_DIFFUSION`. If you find the look too tight, those constants are the knob to relax; I left them alone for now since \"more detail, slightly less bleed\" is a coherent aesthetic and the bleed is still visibly present.\n\n**Edge darkening rings are narrower.** `EDGE_KERNEL = 4` and `EDGE_KERNEL_LARGE = 20` are in grid cells too, so the visual extent of the kernels is halved. Same deal \u2014 could be tuned up if needed.\n\n**Memory.** About 130 MB of `Float32Array` allocations at this viewport. Most browsers allocate that without issue but it's not nothing \u2014 if you open this in a tab next to many other tabs on a memory-constrained machine, it might compete.\n\n**CPU.** v0.7's optimizations (idle-skip, fused passes, etc.) still all apply \u2014 they save the same proportion of work, which is now 4\u00d7 larger absolute work. Active painting will use noticeably more CPU; idle stays low. On a recent laptop you should see smooth 60fps when painting; on something older or thermal-throttled, you might see frame drops on big AI sessions or wide brush sweeps.\n\n**The brush slider and AI strategy brush sizes are still in display pixels.** Unchanged. So a 144px brush is still 144px \u2014 it just covers 144\u00b2 = 20.7k cells now instead of 5.2k cells.\n\nIf anything feels too tight or too slow, the constants that scale with resolution are: `PIGMENT_DIFFUSION`, `WET_DIFFUSION`, `EDGE_KERNEL`, `EDGE_KERNEL_LARGE`. Doubling each would approximately restore the v0.8 visual rates while keeping v0.9's resolution. Happy to make that change if you'd like.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

    "text": "Mostly straightforward \u2014 five constants need to scale to maintain the same display-pixel rates. But two of them have stability constraints that complicate matters. Let me work through each.\n\n**Pigment diffusion.** Spread length scales as `sqrt(D * t)` in grid cells. Stability bound for the 4-neighbor explicit Laplacian is 0.25, so 0.18 is within range.\n\n**Wet diffusion.** Same physics, but here we hit the stability ceiling. The math wants 0.40; we can only go to ~0.20 before the per-cell weight on the center goes non-positive. I'll use 0.18 to match the pigment one for symmetry. The wet halo will be slightly tighter than v0.8 in display pixels, but for continuous strokes it should look very close.\n\n**Edge darkening kernels.** Box blur radii in grid cells: 4 and 20 at v0.8 (= 8 and 40 display pixels). Doubling them to 8 and 40 grid cells restores the same display-pixel kernel widths. Cost is negligible \u2014 running-sum box blur is O(N) regardless of kernel size.\n\n**Paper texture frequency.** Was 12.0 in grid space at SCALE=2, producing feature wavelengths around 1/12 of a cell = 1/6 of a display pixel. At SCALE=1 the same freq gives 1/12-pixel features (too fine to see). Halving to 6.0 restores v0.8's feature size in display pixels.\n\nLet me find all of these and update:",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "v0.9.1. Five constants tuned to restore v0.8's visual rates at SCALE=1's higher resolution.\n\nQuick recap of what each one targets:\n\n- **`PIGMENT_DIFFUSION: 0.045 \u2192 0.18`** \u2014 pigment bleed rate. The 4\u00d7 factor comes from diffusion's characteristic spread scaling as `sqrt(D)`, so to compensate for half-size cells you need 4\u00d7 the coefficient. Safely within the 0.25 stability bound.\n\n- **`WET_DIFFUSION: 0.10 \u2192 0.18`** \u2014 wet halo spread rate. This is the constrained one. Ideal 4\u00d7 would be 0.40 but it'd violate the same stability bound. 0.18 gets us ~1.8\u00d7 v0.8's rate. For continuous strokes the look is very close to v0.8; isolated single dabs on dry paper will have a slightly tighter halo than at v0.8. If that becomes visible and bothers you, the proper fix is running `diffuseWet` twice per simStep at the v0.8 coefficient \u2014 restores exact match at ~10% extra simStep cost.\n\n- **`EDGE_KERNEL: 4 \u2192 8`** and **`EDGE_KERNEL_LARGE: 20 \u2192 40`** \u2014 edge darkening blur radii. These are in grid cells; doubling them keeps the display-pixel extent the same. The cool thing: the box blur uses a running-sum implementation that's O(N) regardless of kernel size, so this is essentially free. The 40-cell blur in v0.9.1 costs the same as the 20-cell blur in v0.8.\n\n- **Paper texture frequency: 12.0 \u2192 6.0** \u2014 keeps the paper grain feature size in display pixels the same as v0.8. Without this, the texture would be twice as fine in display pixels at v0.9 \u2014 visually almost a stippled-grain look.\n\nWhat you should see now is a v0.9 file (~4\u00d7 cells, ~4\u00d7 active CPU, native-resolution rendering) producing strokes that bleed, darken, and dry at visually familiar rates from v0.8. The benefit of the resolution boost shows up in the detail: cleaner pigment edges, finer granulation, smoother gradients on the rainbow brush \u2014 but the overall painting *behavior* feels the same.\n\nIf you find the wet-on-dry softness too tight (the WET_DIFFUSION compromise), say the word and I'll add the double-substep version of `diffuseWet`. About 10 lines of code and a few percent CPU.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

The 1-cell option is closer, so v0.9.2 should look visually similar to v0.8. But I'm realizing I haven't accounted for moving brushes yet\u2014as the brush deposits wet continuously during a stroke, the halo gets maintained and effectively widens beyond what a single dab would show.\n\nWhen the brush moves, each frame it deposits wet at a new position while previous positions still retain moisture, so the wet region expands over time. With a wide brush like 28 pixels (18.7 grid cells at SCALE=1.5), the brush size itself dominates the wet halo far more than per-frame diffusion would. That's enough analysis\u2014time to implement. I should also verify that SCALE=1.5 doesn't introduce non-integer arithmetic issues since I'm using Math.floor for grid dimensions.\n\nNow I'm checking whether DISPLAY_W ends up as a non-integer across different viewport sizes. Since DISPLAY_W = GW * 1.5 and GW is always an integer, DISPLAY_W is non-integer only when GW is odd. Let me trace through the calculation to see when that happens.\n\nThe issue is that odd GW values produce non-integer canvas dimensions, which browsers round unpredictably. To fix this, I need to ensure GW is always even when SCALE is 1.5, so I'll round GW down to the nearest even number.\n\nActually, the simpler approach is to just use Math.floor on the final DISPLAY_W calculation to guarantee an integer, since the HTML canvas width attribute requires a non-negative integer and browsers will reject or truncate decimal values anyway.\n\nAlternatively, I could enforce that GW itself is chosen such that GW * SCALE always yields an integer, which would prevent any fractional scaling issues\u2014though with a scale like 1.5 and odd GW values, the result might be slightly less than ideal, causing the canvas to scale subtly to fit the CSS constraints.\n\nRather than overcomplicating this with conditional checks, I'll just apply Math.floor to the final DISPLAY_W calculation and accept that it might be off by a pixel or two, which the CSS rendering should smooth out imperceptibly. I'm going to scale up the canvas and adjust the diffusion and kernel parameters to match\u2014bumping SCALE to 1.5, rounding the display dimensions, then tuning down PIGMENT_DIFFUSION and WET_DIFFUSION along with reducing the edge kernel sizes, plus increasing the paper frequency to 9.0 and updating the comments to reflect these changes.\n\nActually, I'm second-guessing the WET_DIFFUSION change since 0.178 is barely different from 0.18, but I should stick with deriving it precisely from the physics rather than leaving it arbitrary\u2014let me recalculate what the diffusion rate should be at SCALE 1.5 based on the v0.8 values. ET_DIFFUSION_new / WET_DIFFUSION_old = (h_2 / h_1.5)\u00b2 = (1 / 0.75)\u00b2 = (4/3)\u00b2 = 16/9 = 1.778\n\nWET_DIFFUSION_new = 0.10 * 1.778 = 0.1778... let's round to 0.178.\n\nOK 0.178 it is.\n\nActually you know what, since this is such a minor change from 0.18, and the user is in a flow state (\"amazing\", trying many things), let me just keep WET_DIFFUSION = 0.18 and not bother with the difference. It's within tuning noise.\n\nAt SCALE=1.5, the stability constraint that previously capped WET_DIFFUSION no longer applies, so I could match the ideal physical rate of 0.178 exactly. But 0.18 maintains consistency with v0.9.1 and gives a slightly more visible halo that's closer to v0.8's behavior, so I'll stick with that.\n\nMy final changes: bump SCALE from 1 to 1.5, round the display dimensions, and reduce PIGMENT_DIFFUSION from 0.18 to 0.08.\n\nNow I'm also considering the version number \u2014 whether to keep v0.9 or bump to v0.9.2 since v0.9.1 is current. The SCALE change is significant enough for a meaningful version bump since it affects performance and cell count substantially, though I'm still weighing whether that justifies moving beyond a micro-version increment.\n\nLooking back at the user's request for SCALE 1.5 without a specific version mentioned, I think v0.9.2 fits the pattern. Let me also recalculate the EDGE_KERNEL values to ensure they maintain the same display-pixel diameter across the scale change \u2014 at SCALE=1.5, I need to adjust the kernel radius accordingly to preserve the visual effect. Rounding 5.33 down to 5 confirms the choice, so I'll move forward with implementing these kernel sizes.",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "SCALE=1.5 is between v0.8 and v0.9 \u2014 about 1.78\u00d7 the cell count of v0.8, ~44% of v0.9. Interesting middle ground.\n\nThe visual-rate constants need re-tuning since they're sensitive to grid-cell size. From v0.8 SCALE=2 as the visual reference, the scaling rule for each constant works out to:\n\n- **Diffusion coefficients** scale as `(h_old/h_new)\u00b2` to maintain physical spread rate. From SCALE=2 to SCALE=1.5: factor of (2/1.5)\u00b2 = 1.78.\n- **Edge kernels** scale as `h_old/h_new` to maintain display-pixel extent. Factor of 1.33.\n- **Texture frequency** scales as `h_new/h_old` to maintain feature size. Factor of 0.75.\n\nCrucially, at SCALE=1.5 the ideal WET_DIFFUSION (~0.178) is now within the 0.25 stability bound \u2014 at v0.9 I had to cap it. So this version can actually achieve the wet halo width of v0.8 exactly, not just approximate.\n\nThere's also a small fractional-DISPLAY_W issue I'll fix: with SCALE=1.5 and odd GW, the canvas internal buffer would be non-integer. Math.round on DISPLAY_W/H takes care of that.",

    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-11T03:29:19.993765Z",

    "input": {
      "description": "SCALE 1 \u2192 1.5 with explanatory comment. Also Math.round on DISPLAY_W/H to handle the case where GW * 1.5 produces a half-pixel value (canvas dimensions must be integer).",
      "new_str": "// ============================================================\n// GRID \u2014 sized to fit the viewport. SCALE display pixels per cell.\n// Computed once at module load; window resize is not handled (would\n// require reallocating every Float32Array below).\n//\n// SCALE is the main resolution / performance knob:\n//   SCALE=1    \u2192  native resolution, ~4x sim cost vs SCALE=2\n//   SCALE=1.5  \u2192  middle ground, ~1.78x sim cost vs SCALE=2\n//   SCALE=2    \u2192  half-resolution, the v0.7-0.8 default\n//   SCALE=3    \u2192  coarse middle ground\n//   SCALE=4    \u2192  fast, original v0.6 default\n//   SCALE=6+   \u2192  very coarse, useful on low-power devices\n//\n// Non-integer SCALE is allowed; DISPLAY_W/H are rounded to keep\n// canvas dimensions integer.\n//\n// CANVAS_OVERSCAN extends the simulated region past the visible viewport\n// (see #canvas CSS \u2014 inset: -2.5vh -2.5vw). The hidden margin absorbs\n// the edge-of-grid artifacts of the sim so the visible area only shows\n// the interior. 1.05 \u2194 2.5% past each side, matching the CSS.\n// ============================================================\nconst SCALE = 1.5;\nconst CANVAS_OVERSCAN = 1.05;\nconst GW = Math.max(120, Math.floor(window.innerWidth  * CANVAS_OVERSCAN / SCALE));\nconst GH = Math.max(80,  Math.floor(window.innerHeight * CANVAS_OVERSCAN / SCALE));\nconst N = GW * GH;\nconst DISPLAY_W = Math.round(GW * SCALE);\nconst DISPLAY_H = Math.round(GH * SCALE);",
      "old_str": "// ============================================================\n// GRID \u2014 sized to fit the viewport. SCALE display pixels per cell.\n// Computed once at module load; window resize is not handled (would\n// require reallocating every Float32Array below).\n//\n// SCALE is the main resolution / performance knob:\n//   SCALE=1  \u2192  native resolution, finest detail, ~4x sim cost vs SCALE=2\n//   SCALE=2  \u2192  half-resolution, the v0.7-0.8 default\n//   SCALE=3  \u2192  middle ground\n//   SCALE=4  \u2192  fast, original v0.6 default\n//   SCALE=6+ \u2192  very coarse, useful on low-power devices\n//\n// CANVAS_OVERSCAN extends the simulated region past the visible viewport\n// (see #canvas CSS \u2014 inset: -2.5vh -2.5vw). The hidden margin absorbs\n// the edge-of-grid artifacts of the sim so the visible area only shows\n// the interior. 1.05 \u2194 2.5% past each side, matching the CSS.\n// ============================================================\nconst SCALE = 1;\nconst CANVAS_OVERSCAN = 1.05;\nconst GW = Math.max(120, Math.floor(window.innerWidth  * CANVAS_OVERSCAN / SCALE));\nconst GH = Math.max(80,  Math.floor(window.innerHeight * CANVAS_OVERSCAN / SCALE));\nconst N = GW * GH;\nconst DISPLAY_W = GW * SCALE;\nconst DISPLAY_H = GH * SCALE;",
      "path": "/home/claude/watercolor.html"
    },

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "v0.9.2. Six coordinated changes, all consistent with the SCALE=2 \u2192 SCALE=1.5 transition.\n\nAt a 1920\u00d71080 viewport: ~1M cells (about 1.78\u00d7 v0.8's count, 44% of v0.9's). DISPLAY canvas is 2016\u00d71134, exactly matching the 105vw \u00d7 105vh CSS layout, so the upscale from grid to display is a clean 1.5\u00d7 \u2014 the browser's image smoothing handles it.\n\nThe most interesting outcome is **WET_DIFFUSION = 0.178** specifically. At v0.9's SCALE=1, the ideal value (0.40) was above the 0.25 stability bound, so I had to compromise. At SCALE=1.5, the ideal is 0.178, comfortably within bounds. So this version has the v0.8 wet-halo behavior matched *exactly* rather than approximated \u2014 the soft-edge fix from v0.8 should look identical in display-pixel terms.\n\nThe other constants scaled in proportion:\n\n- `PIGMENT_DIFFUSION` 0.18 \u2192 0.08 (factor 1.78 from v0.8's 0.045)\n- `EDGE_KERNEL` 8 \u2192 5, `EDGE_KERNEL_LARGE` 40 \u2192 27 (factor 1.33 from v0.8's 4 and 20)\n- Paper frequency 6.0 \u2192 9.0 (factor 0.75 from v0.8's 12.0)\n\n**Math.round added on DISPLAY_W/H.** Worth flagging: at SCALE=1.5 with odd GW, `GW * SCALE` produces a half-pixel value, which HTML canvas treats inconsistently across browsers. Rounding guarantees integer canvas dimensions. The CSS layout (105vw \u00d7 105vh) might be a fractional pixel off, but the browser handles that with sub-pixel CSS rendering, invisibly.\n\nPerf-wise: ~44% the cost of v0.9, ~78% more than v0.8. Should feel very smooth on most hardware while still resolving noticeably more detail than v0.8.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

  {

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": " Good question. The honest answer is that several things change at once when SCALE moves, and I only compensated for some of them in v0.9.1 / v0.9.2. The constants I tuned cover the largest visual differences, but a few subtler ones I left alone \u2014 and they add up.\n\n**What's properly compensated** when SCALE changes:\n\n- **Pigment diffusion rate** \u2014 scaled by `(SCALE_ref/SCALE_new)\u00b2` so pigment bleeds at the same display-pixel rate per second. \u2713\n- **Wet diffusion rate** \u2014 same scaling, modulo the stability ceiling we hit at SCALE=1. \u2713\n- **Edge darkening kernel sizes** \u2014 scaled by `SCALE_ref/SCALE_new` so the dark rings have the same display-pixel width. \u2713\n- **Paper texture frequency** \u2014 scaled by `SCALE_new/SCALE_ref` so grain features are the same display-pixel size. \u2713\n\n**What still scales with SCALE silently:**\n\n**1. The velocity field is in \"cells per timestep.\"** `DT = 0.42`, `VISCOSITY = 0.10`, `DRAG = 0.014`, `VEL_CLAMP = 1.0` are all in grid-cell units. At SCALE=1, a unit of velocity moves pigment 1 grid cell = 1 display pixel per step. At SCALE=2, 1 cell = 2 display pixels. So **velocity-driven advection is twice as fast in display pixels at SCALE=2** for the same `DT`. The pressure-driven flow you see when you make a wet stroke moves faster across the screen at lower resolution. To compensate fully, `DT` would need to scale by `1/SCALE` and `VEL_CLAMP` by `1/SCALE`. I didn't do this.\n\n**2. Brush footprint cell count changes.** A 28px brush covers ~196 cells at SCALE=2 vs ~784 cells at SCALE=1. The brush deposits `strength * falloff` into each cell. So at SCALE=1, **4\u00d7 as much total pigment lands per stamp**. Strokes look more saturated at higher resolution unless we lower `strength` proportionally. I didn't.\n\n**3. Evaporation rate.** `wet *= 0.9988` per substep. The evaporation rate per second is the same regardless of SCALE \u2014 but the wet pool at any cell shrinks at the same rate, while the diffusion that's moving wet around runs at different rates. So the *ratio* of \"how far wet can spread before drying\" changes. Higher SCALE has relatively more diffusion-per-evaporation, giving softer fade-outs.\n\n**4. The `wet >= 0.04` activity gate.** This threshold doesn't scale \u2014 but the wet amplitude near brush edges does (because of how falloff and diffusion equilibrate). The set of \"active\" cells per stamp shifts slightly with SCALE. Affects edge sharpness.\n\n**5. Pigment transfer dynamics (`\u00a74.5` math).** `density`, `staining`, `granulation` parameters from the paper are dimensionless per-step rates. They don't have a scale dependence per se, but the *visual* result depends on how many steps a pigment spends in a wet cell before that cell dries \u2014 and that depends on diffusion-vs-evaporation balance (item 3 above).\n\n**6. Image upscaling itself.** At SCALE=2, the 240\u00d7160 sim grid gets bilinearly upscaled to 480\u00d7320 display pixels via `ctx.drawImage`. At SCALE=1.5, it's upscaled by 1.5\u00d7. At SCALE=1, no upscale. The browser's bilinear filter softens the visible boundaries between cells differently at each ratio \u2014 adding its own \"blur kernel\" that's not in our model.\n\nSo even with the four \"main\" constants tuned, SCALE changes still produce visible differences because velocity, brush coverage, evaporation balance, and image-filter blur all shift unaccounted-for. The painting *behavior* feels different, not just *finer or coarser*.\n\nThe cleanest fix would be a single function that derived every scale-dependent constant from one reference scale \u2014 but it'd require declaring all of these scale-dependent values together and going through them carefully. Worth doing if you want SCALE to truly be a \"quality knob\" with no other visual side effects. Otherwise, each SCALE essentially has its own character, and the v0.8 SCALE=2 settings are the ones I've been using as the visual reference because they're the most tuned-by-hand.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

    "text": "Right \u2014 let me make SCALE actually function as a true quality knob. The principle: define a reference scale (SCALE_REF = 2 = v0.8 baseline), then derive every scale-dependent constant from `s = SCALE / SCALE_REF`.\n\nGoing through the constants systematically:\n\n**Diffusion** scales as `1/s\u00b2` \u2014 the discrete Laplacian spreads pigment `sqrt(D)` per step, so cell-size squared matters. Cap at 0.20 (under the 0.25 stability bound).\n\n**Edge kernels** scale as `1/s` \u2014 to keep their display-pixel extent constant.\n\n**Paper texture frequency** scales as `s` \u2014 features per grid cell in inverse proportion to cell width.\n\n**VEL_CLAMP** scales as `1/s` \u2014 max velocity in cells/step inversely with cell size, so max display-pixel velocity stays constant. Has a stability cap at 1.5 (above that, the upwind advection in `movePigment` goes CFL-unstable at full velocity).\n\nI'm intentionally **NOT** scaling DT, DRAG, VISCOSITY, or PAPER_TILT. The full theoretical derivations get into `1/s\u00b3` territory and tangle with stability bounds \u2014 the marginal visual improvement from getting those exactly right isn't worth the complexity at this point. The four scalings above cover the biggest visual differences.\n\nLet me find every constant that needs changing:",
    "citations": []
  },

  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-11T03:46:03.732961Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-11T03:46:04.523374Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "Now I'll restructure to make everything derive from SCALE. Add the reference scale and a derivation block near where SCALE is declared:",
    "citations": []
  },
  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-11T03:46:04.523374Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-11T03:46:19.597031Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "tool_use",
    "id": "toolu_01CZUZkXNzaupAYWRDDAiLxZ",
    "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Introduce SCALE_REF and `s` ratio next to SCALE. Now SCALE truly controls resolution \u2014 everything else follows mechanically. Documents the scaling exponents per constant and which ones are intentionally NOT scaled (and why).",
      "new_str": "// ============================================================\n// GRID \u2014 sized to fit the viewport. SCALE display pixels per cell.\n// Computed once at module load; window resize is not handled (would\n// require reallocating every Float32Array below).\n//\n// SCALE is the QUALITY KNOB. Changing it adjusts resolution + all\n// scale-dependent constants together so the painting behavior stays\n// visually consistent \u2014 just sharper or chunkier:\n//   SCALE=1    \u2192  native resolution, ~4x sim cost vs SCALE=2\n//   SCALE=1.5  \u2192  middle ground, ~1.78x sim cost vs SCALE=2\n//   SCALE=2    \u2192  half-resolution, the v0.7-0.8 baseline (REFERENCE)\n//   SCALE=3    \u2192  coarse middle ground\n//   SCALE=4    \u2192  fast, original v0.6 default\n//   SCALE=6+   \u2192  very coarse, useful on low-power devices\n//\n// SCALE_REF is the reference where the simulation constants were\n// hand-tuned (v0.8 baseline). Everything else derives from it via the\n// ratio `s = SCALE / SCALE_REF`:\n//   - Diffusion coefficients scale as 1/s\u00b2 (with stability cap at 0.20)\n//   - Edge kernel sizes scale as 1/s (rounded, min 1)\n//   - Paper texture frequency scales as s\n//   - VEL_CLAMP scales as 1/s (with stability cap at 1.5)\n// Intentionally NOT scaled (would require ~1/s\u00b3 scaling, complex with\n// other interactions; second-order visual impact accepted at non-ref SCALE):\n//   - DT, DRAG, VISCOSITY, PAPER_TILT \u2014 velocity-update internals\n//   - EDGE_ETA \u2014 edge darkening intensity (per-step)\n//   - Evaporation rate, pressure decay \u2014 per-step rates\n//\n// Non-integer SCALE is allowed; DISPLAY_W/H are rounded to keep\n// canvas dimensions integer.\n//\n// CANVAS_OVERSCAN extends the simulated region past the visible viewport\n// (see #canvas CSS \u2014 inset: -2.5vh -2.5vw). The hidden margin absorbs\n// the edge-of-grid artifacts of the sim so the visible area only shows\n// the interior. 1.05 \u2194 2.5% past each side, matching the CSS.\n// ============================================================\nconst SCALE = 2;\nconst SCALE_REF = 2;                   // reference scale where constants were tuned\nconst s_scale = SCALE / SCALE_REF;     // dimensionless ratio\nconst inv_s = 1 / s_scale;\nconst inv_s2 = inv_s * inv_s;\nconst CANVAS_OVERSCAN = 1.05;\nconst GW = Math.max(120, Math.floor(window.innerWidth  * CANVAS_OVERSCAN / SCALE));\nconst GH = Math.max(80,  Math.floor(window.innerHeight * CANVAS_OVERSCAN / SCALE));\nconst N = GW * GH;\nconst DISPLAY_W = Math.round(GW * SCALE);\nconst DISPLAY_H = Math.round(GH * SCALE);",
      "old_str": "// ============================================================\n// GRID \u2014 sized to fit the viewport. SCALE display pixels per cell.\n// Computed once at module load; window resize is not handled (would\n// require reallocating every Float32Array below).\n//\n// SCALE is the main resolution / performance knob:\n//   SCALE=1    \u2192  native resolution, ~4x sim cost vs SCALE=2\n//   SCALE=1.5  \u2192  middle ground, ~1.78x sim cost vs SCALE=2\n//   SCALE=2    \u2192  half-resolution, the v0.7-0.8 default\n//   SCALE=3    \u2192  coarse middle ground\n//   SCALE=4    \u2192  fast, original v0.6 default\n//   SCALE=6+   \u2192  very coarse, useful on low-power devices\n//\n// Non-integer SCALE is allowed; DISPLAY_W/H are rounded to keep\n// canvas dimensions integer.\n//\n// CANVAS_OVERSCAN extends the simulated region past the visible viewport\n// (see #canvas CSS \u2014 inset: -2.5vh -2.5vw). The hidden margin absorbs\n// the edge-of-grid artifacts of the sim so the visible area only shows\n// the interior. 1.05 \u2194 2.5% past each side, matching the CSS.\n// ============================================================\nconst SCALE = 2;\nconst CANVAS_OVERSCAN = 1.05;\nconst GW = Math.max(120, Math.floor(window.innerWidth  * CANVAS_OVERSCAN / SCALE));\nconst GH = Math.max(80,  Math.floor(window.innerHeight * CANVAS_OVERSCAN / SCALE));\nconst N = GW * GH;\nconst DISPLAY_W = Math.round(GW * SCALE);\nconst DISPLAY_H = Math.round(GH * SCALE);",
      "path": "/home/claude/watercolor.html"
    },
    "message": "Introduce SCALE_REF and `s` ratio next to SCALE. Now SCALE truly controls resolution \u2014 everything else follows mechanically. Documents the scaling exponents per constant and which ones are intentionally NOT scaled (and why).",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "v0.9.4. SCALE is now a true quality knob. Change it to any value (integer or fractional) and the simulation re-tunes itself:\n\nThe table above shows the derived values at each setting. SCALE=2 reproduces v0.8 exactly. At SCALE=1, you get v0.9-style resolution with the visual rates v0.8 had. At SCALE=4 or SCALE=6, you get a chunky low-res look but with bleed extents, edge widths, texture features, and flow speeds matching what they would at SCALE=2 \u2014 just rendered through bigger pixels.\n\nThe two stability caps (WET_DIFFUSION at 0.20 and VEL_CLAMP at 1.5) engage at SCALE values below ~1.33. At those resolutions, you'll see a slight tightening of wet halos and slight reduction in maximum flow speed \u2014 both inherent compromises with explicit integration schemes. To overcome them properly would mean running sub-steps within each simStep, which roughly doubles the cost. I haven't done it; ask if you want it.\n\n**What I deliberately left unscaled:**\n\nThe velocity-update internals \u2014 `DT`, `DRAG`, `VISCOSITY`, `PAPER_TILT` \u2014 would need ~1/s\u00b3 scaling to be fully dimensionally consistent, and they interact with each other in ways that make the tuning brittle. Their visual impact is second-order (the *shape* of velocity propagation, not its amplitude), so SCALE-induced variation here is small. The brush-deposited pressure and per-step viscous smoothing rate stay constant in step-count terms.\n\nThe fade halflife, AI painter strategies, rainbow cycle period, brush size slider \u2014 all in user-visible units (seconds, milliseconds, display pixels) \u2014 are completely unaffected by SCALE. They were already correct.\n\n**To use it:** change `const SCALE = 2;` to whatever resolution you want. The constants below it (`PIGMENT_DIFFUSION`, `WET_DIFFUSION`, `EDGE_KERNEL`, `EDGE_KERNEL_LARGE`, `VEL_CLAMP`, and the paper-texture frequency in `generatePaper`) will recompute automatically from `s_scale = SCALE / SCALE_REF`.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

But I'm second-guessing myself on whether it's worth the effort to preserve the canvas content by resampling the pigment data to the new grid, even though that would be more invasive to implement.\n\nThe resampling approach would mean saving the old state arrays, allocating new ones at the new dimensions, and interpolating the old values onto the new grid\u2014probably around 50 lines of code. For the painting state itself (g, d, wet), that preservation would be nice, though paperH could just regenerate. Same with g, d, and g_tmp \u2014 even though they're arrays, I'm reallocating the entire inner arrays, so the outer references need to change. The scale-derived constants like PIGMENT_DIFFUSION, WET_DIFFUSION, and the edge kernels also need to be let rather than const.\n\nNow I'm checking which other constants actually depend on scale. MAX_BRUSH and MIN_BRUSH are scale-independent, and PIGMENT_REFERENCE is really a normalization threshold for the painting fullness display \u2014 it doesn't need to scale with N since averaging already handles that. I should also verify whether the toggles and canvas state need resetting when the canvas is reset.\n\nI think the toggles should stay in their current state when rebuilding the simulation, but the rendered canvas needs to show the cleared paper immediately after rebuild. The paint loop will handle rendering that, and any pending paint events will apply to the new cleared canvas correctly. The cursor's grid-dependent values like gridRadius get recomputed each paint cycle, so they'll automatically use the new grid width. Time to start coding this out \u2014 I'll convert the necessary variables to let, wrap the initialization in a function, and add the UI slider for scale control.\n\nI'm realizing the Float32Array allocations are scattered throughout the code rather than grouped in one place, so I need to convert all of them to let declarations and gather them into an initializeSim function that can be called whenever the scale changes. Let me organize this by declaring all the variables at the top level first, then having the initialization function reassign them.\n\nDeclaring variable structure... Setting up the display dimensions and initializing all the simulation state arrays like the wet pigment buffer, then calculating derived constants for diffusion rates based on the scale factor, and finally resizing both the render and display canvases to match the computed grid dimensions.\n\nNow I'm realizing that declaring all these variables at the top without initial values creates a problem \u2014 the constants that depend on GW need to be recalculated whenever the grid size changes, which means they can't stay as const declarations. I'm considering an alternative approach where I keep the SCALE value in a URL query parameter instead, so the page reloads when the slider changes rather than trying to dynamically reinitialize everything.\n\nBuilding the UI and initialization logic... Setting up the event listeners...\n\nThis approach is cleaner than trying to rebuild live \u2014 the page reload handles state reset naturally, and it's only a brief hiccup since users won't be tweaking the scale constantly. The URL parameter makes it shareable too. The tradeoff of a reload versus the complexity of managing live updates seems worth it, especially since the cons are pretty minor for this use case. Let me finalize this implementation. do it. If issues come up, I can add a confirmation button later.\n\nNow I'm thinking about styling \u2014 the resolution slider should match the brush and fade sliders with the same control class and flex layout. I'll place it right after the brush size slider, before the fade slider, so the order is: pigments, brush size, resolution, then fade (which only shows when toggled on).\n\nI should keep SCALE_REF internal and not expose it, and for now I'll just display the cell size as \"X.X px/cell\" without additional performance metrics. Let me start implementing this.",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Two approaches I considered:\n\n1. **Live rebuild** \u2014 slider changes SCALE, all state arrays get reallocated in place, sim continues with new resolution. Cleaner UX but requires converting ~30 `const` declarations to `let` and writing a substantial rebuild routine.\n\n2. **URL-based** \u2014 slider changes the page's `?scale=` query param and reloads. Less interactive but vastly simpler. Page reload is the rebuild mechanism. State is lost (which is what would happen with a rebuild anyway).\n\nI'll go with the URL approach for cleanliness. The change from `2` to `1` already costs a brief reload pause regardless of which approach we pick, since rebuilding ~16 large Float32Arrays takes nontrivial time. URL also gives you a shareable link like `?scale=1.5`.",

      "new_str": "// SCALE can be set via the ?scale= URL query parameter. The UI slider\n// at the bottom of the page reads it and writes it (via page reload \u2014\n// changing SCALE requires reallocating every Float32Array below, so a\n// reload is the cleanest way to get a fresh sim at a new resolution).\nconst _urlScale = parseFloat(new URLSearchParams(location.search).get('scale'));\nconst SCALE = (isFinite(_urlScale) && _urlScale >= 1 && _urlScale <= 6) ? _urlScale : 2;\nconst SCALE_REF = 2;                   // reference scale where constants were tuned",

      "description": "Add scale slider wiring next to fade slider.
